ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Sep-15-2021 Continued To Nov-23-21 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Host Defendants Notice Of Motion And Motion For A Determination Of Good Faith Settlement
Off Calendar Aug-09-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTs ABYASA KAMDANI, ARIEL ALDRIN, PATRICIA SUYANTO, RIO ATMADJA, SAMUEL HALIM, SHAUN TJAHAJA, and STANLEY SUTANDINATA (Host Defendants') Motion For A Determination Of Good Faith Settlement:

Host defendants' motion to determine good faith settlement is denied without prejudice.

In Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Assocs. (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499, the California Supreme Court held the following factors are relevant in evaluating the good faith of a settlement: (1) a rough approximation of plaintiffs' total recovery and the settlor's proportionate liability; (2) the amount paid in settlement; (3) the allocation of settlement proceeds among plaintiffs; (4) a recognition that a settlor should pay less in settlement than he would if he were found liable after a trial; (5) the financial conditions of the settling defendants, as well as the existence of collusion, fraud, or tortious conduct aimed to injure the interests of non-settling defendants; and (6) defendant's settlement figure must not be grossly disproportionate to what a reasonable person, at the time of the settlement, would estimate the settling defendant's liability to be.

The party asserting the lack of good faith has the burden of proof on that issue. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 877.6(d).) Nevertheless, once non-settlors attack the settlement as lacking good faith, settlors are required to file counteraffidavits to make an evidentiary showing that the settlement was "in the ballpark." Settlors must provide substantial evidence to support a critical assumption as to the nature and extent of their liability. (See Mattco Forge, Inc. v. Arthur Young & Co. (1995) 38 Cal.App.4th 1337, 1348, 1350 at n.6 [substantial evidence necessary to find that settlement is in good faith].) "The ultimate determinant of good faith is whether the settlement is grossly disproportionate to what a reasonable person at the time of settlement would estimate the settlor's liability to be." (City of Grand Terrace v. Superior Court (1987) 192 Cal.App.3d 1251, 1262.)

Here, there is insufficient evidence in the record approximating plaintiff's total recovery and the Court therefore cannot determine whether the settlement is in the ballpark.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)
